summary: The R32 GT-R is a 35+ year old car, so reliability concerns are largely age-related. The RB26DETT engine is extremely strong when maintained, but the ATTESA AWD system and aging electronics need attention.

issues.name: Ceramic Turbo Wheel Failure
issues.description: The factory T28 turbos use ceramic exhaust wheels that become brittle with age and heat cycles. They can shatter at high boost, sending debris into the engine.

issues.name: ATTESA AWD System Issues
issues.description: The ATTESA ET-S electronic AWD system uses aging sensors, solenoids, and an electronic control unit that can malfunction, causing the car to stay in RWD mode.

issues.name: Wiring Harness Deterioration
issues.description: The 35+ year old wiring harness becomes brittle and develops insulation cracks, causing intermittent electrical faults throughout the car.

issues.name: Rust (Japanese Import Cars)
issues.description: R32 GT-Rs are Japanese domestic market cars. Many have rust in the wheel arches, rocker panels, and trunk floor from Japanese road salt.

issues.name: Oil Pump Drive Collar
issues.description: The RB26 oil pump drive collar can spin on the crank, causing sudden loss of oil pressure and catastrophic engine failure.

buyingTips: Inspect thoroughly for rust — especially wheel arches, rocker panels, trunk floor, and under the rear seats
Verify the ceramic turbos haven't shattered — a boost test and visual inspection are essential
Check the ATTESA AWD system — engage AWD and verify front wheel engagement with a diagnostic tool
The RB26DETT engine is extremely strong but requires quality oil (5W-30 synthetic) and regular maintenance
Budget for age-related restoration — any R32 GT-R will need rubber seals, hoses, bushings, and possibly wiring work
